title: The Case Against Clean Code Aesthetics
description: How the obsession with visual polish kills personality in web design.

body.raw:
I have a confession: I'm tired of "clean" design.

Not tired of well-designed things — I appreciate craft as much as anyone. Tired of the **homogeneity** that "clean" has become. Tired of visiting twenty portfolio sites in a row that all look like they were designed by the same person with the same Figma template.

## What "Clean" Actually Means Now

In 2024, "clean design" has become code for:

- White or near-white background
- System font or Inter/Geist
- 8px grid system
- Rounded corners (8px or 12px)
- Subtle shadows
- Lots of whitespace
- Neutral color palette with one accent color

There's nothing wrong with any of these choices individually. But when everyone makes the same choices, you get **visual monoculture**. And monoculture is boring.

## The Personality Vacuum

Here's what gets lost when you optimize purely for "clean":

1. **Texture.** Real surfaces have grain, noise, imperfection. Digital surfaces don't have to be perfectly smooth.
2. **Asymmetry.** The golden ratio exists in nature. Rigid grid alignment exists in spreadsheets.
3. **Surprise.** When everything follows the same pattern, nothing stands out.
4. **Emotion.** Clean design communicates competence. It doesn't communicate joy, curiosity, or playfulness.

## The Rugrats Principle

Have you ever watched Rugrats? The art style is intentionally messy, asymmetric, and textured. It looks like a child's imagination brought to life. It's chaotic, colorful, and deeply expressive.

Now compare that to modern web design. Which one has more personality? Which one is more memorable?

I'm not saying your banking app should look like a Nickelodeon cartoon. Context matters. But if you're building a personal site, a portfolio, an art project — why would you strip away everything that makes it *yours*?

## Finding the Balance

The goal isn't chaos for chaos's sake. It's **intentional imperfection**:

- Use asymmetry, but maintain visual hierarchy
- Add texture, but preserve readability
- Break the grid, but keep the structure
- Be playful, but respect the user's time

The best designs I've seen aren't the cleanest or the messiest. They're the ones where every choice — including the "weird" ones — serves a purpose.

## Start Breaking Rules

If you're a designer or developer reading this, I challenge you: the next personal project you build, break at least three "rules." Use an unexpected font. Add texture. Make something crooked on purpose. See what happens.

Your users might not notice every choice you made. But they'll feel the difference between a site that was *designed* and a site that was *assembled from a kit*.

Make something weird. Make something yours.

title: Why I Build Weird Things
description: On the importance of play, experimentation, and not taking your portfolio too seriously.

body.raw:
Every time I sit down to build something for my portfolio, I face the same temptation: make it clean, make it professional, make it look like every other developer portfolio out there. Dark mode. Sans-serif font. Grid of project cards. Maybe a subtle gradient.

And every time, I resist.

Not because there's anything wrong with clean design. Clean design is hard to do well. But because **my portfolio should feel like me**, and I am not a clean, minimal, corporate-speak kind of person.

## The Problem with Professionalism

There's a pervasive idea in tech that "professional" means "sterile." That your portfolio should look like it was designed by the same agency that did a SaaS dashboard. That personality is risky.

I think the opposite is true. **Personality is memorable.** The portfolio that makes someone say "what the hell is this?" and then spend 10 minutes exploring is infinitely more valuable than the one that gets a polite nod and a closed tab.

## What Play Produces

When you build things for fun — without worrying about whether they'll impress a hiring manager — you learn faster. You try weirder techniques. You combine technologies in unexpected ways. You make mistakes that lead to discoveries.

My Pixel Garden project started because I wondered "what if cellular automata could evolve?" My Voice Canvas came from a late-night experiment with the Web Audio API. Neither was planned as a portfolio piece. Both taught me more than any tutorial.

## The Sandwich Philosophy

I call my approach "sandwich codes" because:

1. **Layers matter.** A good sandwich has texture, contrast, surprise. So does good software.
2. **It's handmade.** You can taste the difference between a hand-assembled sandwich and one made by a machine. Users can feel the difference between handcrafted software and template-driven development.
3. **It's not precious.** Nobody agonizes over the perfect sandwich. You make it, you eat it, you make a different one next time. Software should be the same — build it, ship it, learn from it, build something else.

## Build Weird Things

If you're reading this and you have a half-finished weird project sitting in a repo somewhere, finish it. Ship it. Don't polish it until it's indistinguishable from everything else. Let it be strange. Let it be yours.

The world has enough clean portfolios. It needs more weird ones.
